attributes.stem: A 64-year-old man is being investigated following a presentation with a three month history of change in bowel habit. He reports increased frequency of bowel motions, opening between 1-4 times per day, associated with 3 kg weight loss. Past medical history is significant only for hypertension, for which he takes perindopril. There is no family history of any gastrointestinal problems. Faecal MCS is normal and faecal calprotectin is negative. The physical examination is unremarkable and he is advised to have a colonoscopy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following findings is most likely to be seen on colonoscopy?
attributes.explanation:
Based on the patient's presentation, the condition of most concern that needs to be excluded is a colorectal cancer. Apart from identifying the tumour itself, the colonoscopy finding that would be most in keeping with such a diagnosis would be the presence of adenomatous polyps.

The most likely finding on colonoscopy would be adenomatous polyps. The persistent changes in bowel habits and unintentional weight loss are concerning for colorectal cancer. The presence of adenomatous polyps is a known risk factor for developing colorectal cancer, although not all polyps will progress to cancer. The risk of cancer depends on various factors, including the size, number, and histology of the polyps.

Polyps that are larger than 1 cm (0.4 inches), have a villus component or high-grade dysplasia, or are located in the proximal colon (the right side of the colon) are associated with a higher risk of cancer. Individuals with multiple adenomatous polyps (known as polyposis syndromes) have a higher risk of developing colorectal cancer.

Adenomatous polyps are benign growths that can develop in the lining of the colon or rectum. They are common in individuals over the age of 50 and can be a precursor to colorectal cancer. On colonoscopy, adenomatous polyps can appear as raised, mushroom-like growths, and their presence would prompt biopsy and removal to prevent the development of cancer. Can be a precursor to cancer.

His symptoms could be due to inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) but is less likely with a normal faecal calprotectin. The peak age of onset for both diseases is in the second to fourth decades of life, although they can occur at any age, including in individuals over 60 years old. Pseudopolyps, skip lesions, serpiginous ulcers and cobblestone appearance are all findings that may be seen in individuals with IBD, but they are not specific to IBD and can also be seen in other conditions such as infections, ischaemic colitis, and radiation colitis.

Pseudopolyps are not true polyps, but rather a reactive tissue response to inflammation or injury in the lining of the colon or rectum. They can be seen in individuals with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) such as Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. Pseudopolyps appear as elevated areas of inflamed tissue on colonoscopy.

Skip lesions are areas of the colon or rectum that are unaffected by inflammation or disease in individuals with IBD. They are characteristic of Crohn's disease, which can affect any part of the digestive tract, but typically involves the small intestine and colon.

Serpiginous ulcers are deep, winding ulcers that can develop in individuals with Crohn's disease. They are typically seen in the colon and can cause abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and rectal bleeding.

Cobblestone appearance is a characteristic finding in individuals with Crohn's disease. It refers to the appearance of multiple, raised, and inflamed areas of tissue in the lining of the colon or rectum, which can resemble cobblestones. These areas are separated by deeper ulcerations, giving the affected tissue a lumpy, irregular appearance on colonoscopy.
learningPoints: On colonoscopy, adenomatous polyps can appear as raised, mushroom-like growths, and their presence would prompt biopsy and removal to exclude cancer.
Indications for diagnostic colonoscopy include blood per rectum (37%), abdominal pain (34%) and anaemia (23%).
references: Stauffer CM, Pfeifer C. Colonoscopy. [Updated 2023 Feb 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-.
Lotfollahzadeh S, Recio-Boiles A, Cagir B. Colon Cancer. [Updated 2022 Dec 3]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-.
